title: Forgetful Consumers and Consumption Tracking
abstract: We study the market consequences of advances in consumption tracking technologies—such as mobile banking apps that help consumers monitor their spending and avoid overdrawn accounts—using a two-period consumption model. In the model, consumers pay a penalty fee if they consume in both periods. In the second period, consumers may be forgetful of their first-period consumption, although the use of consumption tracking can remind them. According to our analysis, the availability of consumption tracking often helps consumers at the expense of the firm; such benefits may bedirect, where consumers make use of the technology to avoid penalty fees, orindirect, where the mere availability of consumption tracking forces the firm to lower its penalty fee. If consumers arepartiallysophisticated regarding their forgetfulness, however, the availability of consumption tracking may instill a false sense of security in that consumersexpectto use consumption tracking to avoid penalty fees but ultimately, decide not to bother, making them especially susceptible to penalty fees. In some cases, the availability of consumption tracking may actually compel a firm to impose a penalty fee that would not otherwise be viable, leading to higher profits and lower consumer surplus. As we show, this scenario is attained within an intermediate range of forgetfulness and at a level of (partial) sophistication for which consumers overestimate their demand for the technology.

author.Ying Bao: Gies College of Business, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, Champaign, Illinois 61820
author.Peter Landry: Department of Management, University of Toronto, Mississauga, Ontario L5L 1C6, Canada
author.Mengze Shi: Department of Marketing, HKUST Business School, Hong Kong

article.1. Introduction: Penalty fees are prevalent in many industries. As one example, U.S. banks collected over 15 billion dollars in penalty fees from overdrawn bank accounts in 2019 alone (Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 2021). Similarly, cellular service contracts often impose penalties for subscribers who exceed their monthly allotment of minutes and/or data. Empirical research suggests that penalty fees are often accrued as a result of consumers’forgetfulness. In a BritishOffice of Fair Trading (2008)survey, only 7% of consumers who were charged penalty fees for overdrawn bank accounts expressed that “they knew it would happen but had to make a payment.” Instead, most indicated that they lost track of their spending and incorrectly believed that they had enough remaining funds to avoid overdrawing their accounts.1Furthermore, empirical research suggests that consumers tend tounderestimatethe extent of their forgetfulness (e.g., in remembering to claim rebates (Marzilli Ericson 2011,Rodemeier 2021) and in scheduling dental appointments (Altmann et al. 2022)).2In markets with penalty fees, consumers’ lack of “sophistication” in this sense would naturally make them less cautious in their initial use of a service—and by extension,especiallyvulnerable to penalty fees. The recent proliferation of consumption tracking technologies—such as mobile banking apps that help consumers monitor their spending—represents a potentially promising and significant development for consumers who struggle to avoid penalty fees. However, it remains to be seen whether (or to what extent) these technologies will deliver on their promise. Indeed, it is not entirely clear how consumption tracking technologies may impact market behavior and outcomes. For instance, will consumers actually make use of these technologies? How will service providers respond? Will penalty fees be eliminated? This paper analytically investigates the market effects of advances in consumption tracking technologies. In our model, a firm offers a two-period service contract to a consumer market. This contract is defined by a subscription price and a penalty fee that is incurred by consumers who use the service in both periods. Importantly, consumers may be forgetful of their first-period usage by the time the second period arrives; in addition, consumers may only bepartiallysophisticated in the sense that they initially underestimate the extent of their forgetfulness. That said, consumersmighthave access to a consumption tracking technology that, if used, reminds them of their first-period usage prior to their second-period decision. To assess the effects of advances in consumption tracking technologies, we compare the market-level predictions of our model with consumption tracking to the predictions of a benchmark model without consumption tracking. (Advances in consumption tracking technology may, in our model, also be conceived as a reduction in the cost of using the technology.) As our analysis illustrates, however, advances in consumption tracking technologies are not necessarily consequential. In particular, such advances have no effect on equilibrium behavior if the cost (or “hassle”) from using the technology is sufficiently high, if consumers have sufficiently reliable memories, and/or if consumers are sufficiently sophisticated regarding their forgetfulness. In these cases, the firm maximizes its profits by offering the same contract that was optimal in the absence of consumption tracking. Turning to the remaining cases in which the availability of consumption trackingisconsequential, our analysis reveals multiple channels through which advances in consumption tracking technologies can help consumers at the expense of the firm. As one possibility, it may be optimal for the firm to reduce (or even eliminate) its penalty fee to prevent consumers from using the technology to avoid penalty fees; in these cases, consumers benefit from the availability of consumption tracking, even though they do not actually use the technology. If the firm does not modify its contract, consumers maydirectlybenefit by making use of the technology to successfully avoid penalty fees. With an especially low cost of using the consumption tracking technology to recall past consumption, however, consumers instead choose to “cancel” their subscriptions. Ironically, these unserved consumers still benefit from the availability of consumption tracking because they overpay for their subscriptions in equilibrium without consumption tracking. The outcomes described naturally fit with an intuition that advances in consumption tracking technologies would generallyhurtthe firm by limiting its ability to profit from penalty fees (although the firm may mitigate its losses by modifying its contract offering). That said, our analysis also reveals conditions under which the availability of consumption tracking can enable the firm toincreaseits profits. Namely, if consumers are moderately sophisticated and with moderate costs of using the consumption tracking technology to recall past consumption, it may be possible for the firm to redesign its contract such that consumersplanto use consumption tracking to avoid penalty fees yet ultimately, they decide not to bother. In these equilibria, the availability of consumption tracking paradoxically makes consumersmorevulnerable to penalty fees—while potentially allowing the firm to earn higher profits—by instilling a false sense of security, whereby consumers expect to make use of the technology to avoid penalty fees but ultimately, fail to follow through.3 Besides accommodating the possibility that consumption tracking can help the firm increase its profits, equilibria in which consumers falsely expect to use consumption tracking can entail other unique properties. For instance, the firm may optimally set a positive penalty fee, even though it would have set its penalty fee to zero if consumption tracking had not been available; in this way, our analysis demonstrates that it may be optimal for a firm to impose anewpenalty fee that was not previously viable. In this case, the availability of consumption tracking leads to lower consumer surplus along with higher profits; as we show, this scenario is realized within an intermediate range of consumer forgetfulness along with a level of (partial) sophistication for which consumers overestimate their demand for the technology. In other cases, it may be optimal for the firm to reduce its penalty fee relative to its optimal level without consumption tracking while still collecting more total revenue from penalty fees and earning higher total profits as a result of consumers being more vulnerable to penalty fees based on their false expectation that they will use consumption tracking to recall past consumption. Interestingly, however, consumers are not necessarily hurt in these cases as their overall usage of the service (and hence, the total value derived from their usage) increases. This suggests that the availability of consumption tracking technologies can be mutually beneficial to consumers and the firm—although this can only happen if it gives consumers false hope that they will use consumption tracking to avoid penalty fees. Although there is relatively little research investigating the market effects of advances in consumption tracking technologies, our study can still be related to multiple existing streams of research. To begin, our study relates to the growing literature on three-part tariffs—where the “third part” of the tariff may be interpreted as a per-unit penalty fee—in service contracts. Most work in this area does not address the use of penalty fees in markets with forgetful consumers (e.g.,Sundararajan 2004,Lambrecht et al. 2007,Ascarza et al. 2012,Bagh and Bhargava 2013,Herweg and Mierendorff 2013,Leider and Şahin 2014,Fibich et al. 2017,Desai et al. 2018,Guo 2023); that said, there are some important exceptions (Grubb 2015,Grubb and Osborne 2015,Liu et al. 2018,Chen et al. 2022). The most closely related of these works isGrubb (2015). As in our model,Grubb (2015)considers consumers who are forgetful (or “inattentive”) toward their past consumption in markets with penalty fees. Unlike our focus on the market-level consequences of advances in consumption tracking technologies, however,Grubb (2015)addresses an entirely different research question by focusing on the effects of “bill-shock” regulations that require firms to alert consumers who are on the verge of incurring penalty fees. These alerts may indeed be implemented through technologies (such as automated text messages) that have much in common with the consumption tracking technologies considered in our paper. That said, a regulator determines whether to implement these alerts, which—as modeled by inGrubb (2015)—effectively force all consumers to use consumption tracking (at zero cost); by contrast, consumers in our model endogenously choose forthemselveswhether to incur a cost (e.g., a hassle cost) to use consumption tracking. Another differentiating feature of our study is that we considerpartiallysophisticated consumers who know they are forgetful but underestimate the extent of their forgetfulness, whereasGrubb (2015)only considers consumers who are fully naive or fully sophisticated. This distinction may at first seem modest in comparison with the differences discussed. However, our consideration of partially sophisticated consumers—which as noted earlier, is empirically well supported—proves highly consequential in our analysis. For instance, we find that consumers must be partially sophisticated for the firm to earn higher profits as a result of advances in consumption tracking technologies. Similarly, advances in consumption tracking technologies can only lead a firm to impose a new penalty fee (that would not otherwise exist) and can only be mutually beneficial to consumers and the firm if consumers are partially sophisticated. At the heart of these unique market outcomes is the possibility that partially sophisticated consumers mayexpectto use the consumption tracking technology yet decide against it when the opportunity arrives; importantly, only partially sophisticated consumers can hold such false expectations as fully sophisticated consumers would always follow through if they expect to use consumption tracking, whereas fully naive consumers would never expect to use consumption tracking in the first place. Our work also relates to the empirical analyses ofGrubb and Osborne (2015)andChen et al. (2022). As in our study, these studies consider optimal contract design in markets with penalty fees. That said, these studies do not address consumers’ potential forgetfulness of past consumption—not to mention the possibility that consumers may be partially sophisticated with regard to their forgetfulness (although the representation of consumer overconfidence byGrubb and Osborne (2015)does resemble our representation of naivete). These studies also followGrubb (2015)in focusing on the effects of bill-shock regulation as opposed to our focus on the effects of advances in consumption tracking technologies. Although the market effects of advances in consumption tracking technologies are not explored, theLiu et al. (2018)empirical analysis of banking overdraft provides many relevant insights for understanding consumers’ responses to penalty fees. For instance, the findings ofLiu et al. (2018)highlight the importance of the costs of using consumption tracking technologies in deterring consumers from monitoring their bank accounts. This is also a recurring theme in our analysis; as we find, the firm will often leverage this effect by strategically setting its penalty fee at a level that ensures a consumer’s incentive to use the technology is insufficient to overcome its cost. Another noteworthy finding from the study ofLiu et al. (2018)is that consumers who frequently overdraft their bank accounts are disproportionately likely to “front-load” their spending during a given pay period. This tendency is indeed captured by our model. In addition to the (relatively few) studies that have addressed consumer forgetfulness of past consumption in markets with penalty fees, researchers in marketing and economics have modeled other forms of forgetfulness in consumer decision making. For instance, several studies have explored markets with consumers who are forgetful of past prices (e.g.,Helgeson and Beatty 1987,Dickson and Sawyer 1990,Chen et al. 2010). As another approach, others have modeled markets with consumers who may forget to consider a particular product (e.g.,Sahni 2015,Lovett and Staelin 2016,Landry 2022). Others, meanwhile, have instead studied consumers who are forgetful in their evaluations of products and/or brands (e.g.,Mehta et al. 2004,Shapiro 2006,Villas-Boas and Villas-Boas 2008). The rest of the paper is organized as follows. InSection 2, we describe the model. InSection 3, we analyze a benchmark version of our model without consumption tracking. We then analyze the market effects of consumption tracking inSection 4. InSection 5, we consider variations and extensions of our model (and discuss which results of our main analysis are still attained). InSection 6, we elaborate on the managerial and policy implications of our study and address directions for future research.
article.2. The Model: We consider a market with a monopoly firm and a unit mass of consumers. Att= 0, the firm offers a service contract, and consumers then decide whether to subscribe to the service. A subscription allows a consumer to use (consume) the firm’s service in each of two consumption periods,t= 1, 2. At each consumption periodt, a consumer learns her current valuation of the servicevt, which is independently drawn from a uniform distribution between zero and one, and then decides whether to consume. We let𝑑𝑡∈{0,1}dt∈{0,1}denote the consumer’s periodtconsumption choice,𝐷𝑡≡Pr⁡[𝑑𝑡=1]Dt≡Pr⁡[dt=1]denote the (ex ante) probability of consuming in periodt, and𝐷12≡Pr⁡[𝑑1=𝑑2=1]D12≡Pr⁡[d1=d2=1]denote the probability of consuming in both consumption periods. Here, we are following the precedents ofGrubb (2015)in modeling a binary consumption choice made in two periods with temporally independent valuations. Although crude, a two-period, binary-choice consumption model arguably provides the simplest possible framework that allows us to meaningfully study the market effects of advances in consumption tracking technologies with forgetful consumers. The service contract has two components. The first is a subscription price,p, which is paid by a consumer who subscribes to the service regardless of her usage in subsequent consumption periods. The second is a penalty fee,𝜙ϕ, which is paid by a consumer who consumes in both consumption periods. Thus, with𝜙>0ϕ>0, we can think of a subscription as providing one unit of consumption, with a penalty for consuming in excess of this allocation. (Because a consumer effectively payspfor one unit of consumption or𝑝+𝜙p+ϕfor two units of consumption, the firm may also be understood as adopting a quantity surcharge strategy if𝜙>𝑝ϕ>por a quantity discount strategy if𝜙<𝑝ϕ<p.) We assume (for simplicity) that all market participants are perfectly patient (i.e., no discounting) and that the marginal cost of providing the service is zero. The firm’s expected profit from a consumer who subscribes to the service is then Besides thet= 0 subscription decision and thet= 1, 2 consumption decisions, a consumer may face one additional decision. Namely, upon arriving att= 2 but before learningv2, a consumer with access to a consumption tracking technology decides whether to use the technology. If used, the technology allows the consumer to remember, with certainty, whether she consumed att= 1. However, the use of the technology entails a “monitoring cost,”𝑘≥0k≥0, which can include any time, effort, or psychological costs (and to the extent that it may also include a monetary cost, it is not paid to the firm). Formally, we let𝑑𝜏∈{0,1}dτ∈{0,1}denote whether a given consumer tracks hert= 1 consumption and𝐷𝜏≡Pr⁡[𝑑𝜏=1]Dτ≡Pr⁡[dτ=1]denote the probability that the consumer uses consumption tracking. Such forgetfulness is (for now) modeled in the following way; whent= 2 arrives, a consumer who consumed att= 1 believes that there is a probability𝛼∈(0,1)α∈(0,1)that consumption didnotoccur att= 1. Thus, a higher value ofαmeans that the consumer is moreforgetfulin the sense of assigning a higher probability to the incorrect prospect that she did not consume att= 1. Meanwhile, the consumer is not forgetful of pastabstinence. That is, the consumer will not mistakenly remember consuming after choosing not to consume att= 1; forgetfulness only applies to the tangible experience of a consumption choice.4 Besides allowing imperfect memory throughα, we also allow consumers to imperfectly anticipate their forgetfulness, with̃𝛼∈[0,𝛼]α˜∈[0,α]denoting a consumer’s initial expectation, held prior tot= 2, regarding the true value ofα. Table 1.List of Model Primitives Table 1.List of Model Primitives It is worth acknowledging that our approach to modeling consumer forgetfulness suggests a rather basic mechanism for how, after consuming att= 1, a consumer forms hert= 2 beliefs regarding her prior consumption. Namely, these beliefs are simply given by an exogenous parameterαas opposed to arising through a more “thoughtful” inference. Nonetheless,αmay still be interpreted as the output of an (unmodeled) inference based on mixed memory signals.7As another possibility, the consumer may be able to perfectly infer her past consumption based on internal and/or external signals (e.g., contemplation and receipts), but such inference may entail a cost—unlike the immediate memory signal(s) on which her belief throughαis based. Under this interpretation, a consumer’s efforts to perfectly infer her past consumption would, in essence, represent a crude form of consumption tracking; modern consumption tracking technologies could then be understood as tools that make it easier for the consumer to infer her past consumption (formally represented as a decrease in the monitoring costk). With all that said, later we will consider many alternate approaches to modeling consumer forgetfulness, including several variants in which we explicitly model the inferences underlying consumers’ beliefs of past consumption as well as a variant in which consumers are symmetrically forgetful of past consumption and past abstinence. SeeSection 5for an extended discussion of these alternate models and the Online Appendix for detailed analyses. Before we proceed to our analysis, we recap the timing of decisions as follows. Att= 0, the firm choosespand𝜙ϕ; each consumer then chooses whether to subscribe to the service givenpand𝜙ϕ. Att= 1, a consumer who subscribes to the service learns her present valuationv1and decides whether to consume, as indicated by𝑑1∈{0,1}d1∈{0,1}. Att= 2, if the consumer has access to the consumption tracking technology, she decides whether to incur the monitoring costkto check hert= 1 consumption, as indicated by𝑑𝜏∈{0,1}dτ∈{0,1}; after this (potential) tracking decision, the consumer learns her valuationv2and decides whether to consume, as indicated by𝑑2∈{0,1}d2∈{0,1}.
article.3. Benchmark Analysis Without Consumptio: We first analyze a benchmark version of our model without consumption tracking. In this benchmark model, consumers may be understood as lacking access to consumption tracking (as would be the case before these technologies were developed). As alternate interpretations, it could be that consumption tracking is available but prohibitively costly, that consumers are not (yet) aware of the technology, or that consumers were once aware but have since forgotten that they can use the technology. To begin our benchmark analysis, we first characterize the consumption behavior of a consumer who has subscribed to the service. We then characterize the firm’s optimal contract and resultant equilibrium behavior. 3.1. Consumption Profiles Without Consumption Tracking Assuming she has subscribed to the service, a consumer’s consumption choices depend on her realized valuations of the service (v1andv2), her actual and perceived forgetfulness (αand̃𝛼α˜), and the penalty fee (𝜙ϕ). If there is no penalty fee (𝜙=0ϕ=0), the consumer will always consume in both consumption periods:𝑑1=𝑑2=1d1=d2=1. With a positive penalty fee (𝜙>0ϕ>0), we can use backward induction to characterize consumption behavior, starting with thet= 2 decision. If the consumer did not consume att= 1, then she will always consume att= 2 because she is not at risk for accruing the penalty; thus,𝐷2⁡(𝜙|𝑑1=0)=1D2⁡(ϕ|d1=0)=1for any𝜙ϕ. If she did consume att= 1, she may still consume att= 2 but only if her valuationv2exceeds(1−𝛼)⁢𝜙(1−α)⁢ϕ, which is the penalty fee scaled by her belief of the likelihood of having consumed att= 1; thus,𝐷2⁡(𝜙|𝑑1=1)=Pr⁡[𝑣2>(1−𝛼)⁢𝜙]=max⁡{1−(1−𝛼)⁢𝜙,0}D2⁡(ϕ|d1=1)=Pr⁡[v2>(1−α)⁢ϕ]=max⁡{1−(1−α)⁢ϕ,0}. Naturally, the likelihood of consuming att= 2 in this case is, all else equal, lower if the consumer has a better memory of her past consumption (smallerα) and/or with a larger penalty fee (higher𝜙ϕ). The consumption decision att= 1 then depends onv1as well as the consumer’s expectation of hert= 2 consumption behavior conditional on hert= 1 choice. This implies that her probability of consuming att= 1 depends only oñ𝛼α˜and𝜙ϕ. Generally speaking, the consumer’st= 1 consumption probability is decreasing iñ𝛼α˜—suggesting that the consumer is more cautious when she anticipates a higher level of forgetfulness—but varies nonmonotonically with𝜙ϕ. Intuitively, the consumer is least likely to consume when𝜙ϕis high enough to make the consumer want to avoid the penalty fee but is low enough that she anticipates the possibility of consuming a second time and thus, incurring the penalty fee. Figure 1illustrates the regions depending onαand𝜙ϕ, under which different consumption profiles may arise for naive consumers and for fully sophisticated consumers. As seen in the upper panel ofFigure 1, a naive consumer will never pursue abstinence-based avoidance of the penalty fee; this makes sense considering that abstinence-based avoidance would only be pursued by a consumer who (at least partially) anticipates her future forgetfulness. Meanwhile, as seen in the lower panel ofFigure 1, a fully sophisticated consumer will never unintentionally accrue the penalty fee; this too makes sense as a fully sophisticated consumer would always have accurate expectations concerning her likelihood of consuming in both periods. An interesting insight fromLemma 1is that for a consumer to (mistakenly) consume in both periods and incur a high penalty fee, being forgetful is not enough. With sufficiently high sophistication, a consumer can anticipate this cognitive limitation and abstain from consumption in the first period to avoid such mistakes (as in the case of abstinence-based avoidance). Thus, the level of sophistication (through̃𝛼α˜) is an important determinant of a consumer’s consumption behavior. The following remark summarizes the effect of̃𝛼α˜on consumption in our benchmark model: Without consumption tracking, an increase iñ𝛼α˜(all else equal) leads to a lower probability of consuming in the first period (D1decreases), a higher probability of consuming in the second period (D2increases), and a lower probability of consuming in both periods (D12decreases). In comparison with a less sophisticated consumer, a more sophisticated consumer (with higher̃𝛼α˜) thus tends to back-load her consumption by consuming less (on average) in the first period and more in the second, and this consumer is also less likely to accrue the penalty by consuming in both periods. This suggests a negative association between a consumer’s tendency to accrue penalty fees and a tendency to back-load consumption. Consistent with this prediction,Liu et al. (2018)report that consumers who frequently overdraft their bank accounts also tend tofront-loadconsumption in that they disproportionately spend from their accounts at the beginning (compared with the end) of a pay period. 3.2. Benchmark Equilibria Without Consumption Tracking We now consider the firm’s optimal contract in the benchmark model without consumption tracking. Part (i) ofProposition 1describes azero-penalty benchmark equilibrium, in which the firm sets its penalty fee to zero while extracting all consumer surplus through a relatively high subscription price (𝑝*=E⁢[𝑣1+𝑣2]=1p*=E⁢[v1+v2]=1) that reflects the full expected value from consuming in both periods without penalty. As seen, the zero-penalty benchmark equilibrium arises if consumers have sufficiently reliable memories (i.e., low-enoughα); here, the firm could not increase its profits with a high penalty fee because these consumers would succeed in memory-based avoidance of the penalty. Interestingly, the zero-penalty benchmark equilibrium also arises with more forgetful consumers who are sufficiently sophisticated about their forgetfulness. With fully sophisticated consumers, for instance, the condition for the zero-penalty benchmark equilibrium must hold (because it reduces to𝛼≤1α≤1giveñ𝛼=𝛼α˜=α). These consumers would also successfully avoid a high penalty fee, except through abstinence-based (instead of memory-based) avoidance. Meanwhile, part (ii) ofProposition 1describes apositive-penalty benchmark equilibriumthat arises if consumers are sufficiently forgetful and not too sophisticated about their forgetfulness (i.e.,αmust be sufficiently high relative tõ𝛼α˜).8In this equilibrium, the firm charges a positive penalty fee while offering a lower subscription price than in the zero-penalty benchmark equilibrium. Here, the firm strategically sets its penalty fee at a level that leads consumers tobelievethey will succeed in memory-based avoidance, but in reality, consumers may mistakenly consume in both periods and thus, unintentionally accrue the penalty. As we can see, this positive-penalty equilibrium entails𝜙*𝑁⁢𝑇>1>𝑝*𝑁⁢𝑇ϕN⁢T*>1>pN⁢T*, implying a higher per-unit cost for two units of consumption than for one unit. The firm may thus be understood as employing a quantity surcharge strategy in the positive-penalty benchmark equilibrium; by contrast, the second unit is essentially “free” in the zero-penalty benchmark equilibrium and could thus be interpreted as reflecting a quantity discount strategy. As we can also see inProposition 1, the positive-penalty benchmark equilibrium entails higher profits than the zero-penalty benchmark equilibrium. This makes sense considering that the positive-penalty benchmark equilibrium is only viable when it is possible to improve upon the profits that would be attained without a penalty fee. Here, the potential to earn extra profits stems from the firm’s ability to collectunintentionally accruedpenalty fees. To the firm, it is important that the penalty fees are unintentionally accrued; otherwise, consumers would have to be compensated with an even lower subscription price that reflects the true value of a subscription. In this sense, a consumer overvalues and thus,overpaysfor a subscription in the positive-penalty benchmark equilibrium (𝑝*𝑁⁢𝑇=̃𝑉⁡(𝜙*𝑁⁢𝑇)>𝑉⁡(𝜙*𝑁⁢𝑇)pN⁢T*=V˜⁡(ϕN⁢T*)>V⁡(ϕN⁢T*)), leading tonegativeconsumer surplus (CSNT< 0). The conditions onαinProposition 1suggest that the use of (positive) penalty fees will be more common in contexts where consumers are more forgetful. For example, it is arguably harder—implying higherα—to (mentally) keep track of one’s checking account balance than to keep track of one’s usage of minutes on a calling plan. After all, tracking one’s checking account likely requires remembering a wide range of spending “types”—handwritten checks, ATM withdrawals, debit card purchases (which may be easily confused with credit card purchases), and preauthorized automatic payments (which may have minimal salience and thus, be easily forgotten)—not to mention remembering the varying amounts of each expenditure. By contrast, a consumer’s use of minutes on a calling plan is presumably more consistent, with each additional minute of calling likely to be similar in salience to the previous minute (and involving an easier “plus one0” calculation to track cumulative use). In light of this comparison,Proposition 1would seem to indicate that penalty fees would be more common for checking accounts than for calling plans. Consistent with this prediction, overdraft fees have long been a staple of personal checking accounts, yet “unlimited” calling plans (i.e., without penalty fees) are relatively common.

Effects of Consumption Tracking with the Benchmark Equilibrium Contract Before we characterize the full equilibrium with consumption tracking, it is useful to first examine the potential impacts of consumption tracking given that the firm maintains its benchmark equilibrium contract.10Here, consumption tracking would not affect a consumer’s decisions (or market outcomes more generally) if the monitoring cost from using the technology is large enough that a consumer would never use or expect to use consumption tracking (i.e., if𝑘>max⁡{𝑘𝜏,̃𝑘𝜏}k>max⁡{kτ,k˜τ}) and also, if consumers have sufficiently low forgetfulness (lowα) because the penalty fee would then be zero (fromProposition 1), and consumption tracking serves no purpose without a penalty fee. With a positive penalty fee and a lower monitoring cost, consumption tracking can impact a consumer’s choices in two ways, as shown inFigure 3. First, with low sophistication (low̃𝛼α˜), a consumer may choose to use consumption tracking to remember hert= 1 consumption, ensuring that she never incurs the penalty fee (the pink region inFigure 3). In this case, the consumerdirectlybenefits from the availability of consumption tracking by making use of the technology to avoid penalty fees. Meanwhile, with high sophistication (high̃𝛼α˜), a consumer would instead be motivated to “unsubscribe” from the service (the light gray region inFigure 3). This happens because the consumer correctly anticipates that shewoulduse consumption tracking, but—recalling that consumers already expect to avoid the penalty fee in the benchmark equilibrium without consumption tracking—she perceives her potential future use of consumption tracking as carrying an unnecessary cost withk> 0. As a result, she is no longer willing to pay the price for a subscription. Equilibria with Consumption Tracking We now consider the full market equilibria with consumption tracking, where the firm may now optimally adjust its subscription price and penalty fee (relative to the benchmark levels). In general, the firm would be particularly hurt by the availability of consumption tracking if consumers use the technology to avoid penalty fees or even worse, if it compels consumers to unsubscribe. In all equilibria with consumption tracking, the firm strategically and optimally designs its contract to ensure that consumers still subscribe but do not actually use consumption tracking.11That said, these equilibria may differ in terms of whether consumers’ nonuse of consumption tracking is “planned.” In aplanned (nonuse) equilibrium, consumers neither use nor expect to use consumption tracking (̃𝐷𝜏=𝐷𝜏=0D˜τ=Dτ=0); in anunplanned (nonuse) equilibrium, consumers initially expect to use consumption tracking but ultimately, decide not to bother (̃𝐷𝜏>𝐷𝜏=0D˜τ>Dτ=0). A closer look at part (i) ofProposition 2reveals that planned equilibria can be divided into two types. With a sufficiently large monitoring cost, the firm maintains its benchmark equilibrium contract; in this case, the equilibrium is entirely identical to the benchmark equilibrium that would arise without consumption tracking (which naturally entails planned nonuse because consumers neither use nor expect to use consumption tracking when it is not available). With a lower monitoring cost, however, any planned equilibrium will feature a new contract with a lower penalty fee (𝜙*<𝜙*𝑁⁢𝑇ϕ*<ϕN⁢T*). In these cases, consumerswouldbe willing to use consumption tracking if the firm maintained its (positive) penalty fee from the benchmark equilibrium; to prevent this, the firm strategically reduces (and possibly eliminates) its penalty fee to a point where consumers are no longer inclined to use consumption tracking.12Although consumers do not actually use the technology in a planned equilibrium, they still benefit (at the expense of the firm) from the availability of consumption tracking because it forces the firm to reduce its penalty fee. The question of whether the equilibrium with consumption tracking entails planned or unplanned nonuse depends on the parametersα,̃𝛼α˜, andk. The exact role of each parameter, however, is not entirely clear from the boundary conditions inProposition 2, which are largely expressed in terms of measures (𝜙𝜏,̃𝜙𝜏ϕτ,ϕ˜τ, and̂𝛼α^) that only implicitly depend onα,̃𝛼α˜, and/ork. The next two remarks help clarify the nature of these relationships, whereas the equilibrium regions (for a given value ofk) are illustrated inFigure 4.13 A planned equilibrium that is identical to the benchmark equilibrium is guaranteed with a sufficiently high monitoring cost (k), sufficiently low consumer forgetfulness (α), and/or full sophistication (̃𝛼=𝛼α˜=α). It is not surprising that the benchmark equilibrium is maintained with a prohibitively high monitoring cost as the availability of consumption tracking would effectively be irrelevant in this case. It also makes sense that the benchmark equilibrium is maintained if consumers have sufficiently low forgetfulness or are fully sophisticated because in these cases, the firm would optimally set its penalty fee to zero, regardless of the availability of consumption tracking.14 A planned equilibrium (although not necessarily identical to the benchmark equilibrium) is also guaranteed with a sufficiently low monitoring cost (k) and/or sufficiently low consumer sophistication (small-enough̃𝛼α˜relative toα). Naturally, consumers would be willing to use consumption tracking in the presence of a positive penalty fee if the cost of using the technology is sufficiently low; thus, the firm is forced to eliminate its penalty fee, in which case consumers will neither use nor plan to use consumption tracking in equilibrium. A planned nonuse equilibrium must also arise with sufficientlyunsophisticated consumers because these consumers falsely believe that they do not need consumption tracking to accurately remember their past consumption and thus, would never expect to use consumption tracking. Taken together,Remarks 2and3imply that anunplannedequilibrium—where consumers expect to use consumption tracking but do not actually use it as described in part (ii) ofProposition 2—can only arise when consumers arepartiallysophisticated with regard to their (relatively high) forgetfulness. This makes sense considering that some level of sophistication is necessary for consumers to realize that they could benefit from the use of consumption tracking, yet full sophistication would preclude the possibility that a consumer’s expected use and actual use of consumption tracking could differ. One unique aspect of unplanned equilibria is that the firm’s profits may behigherthan in the benchmark equilibrium without consumption tracking. That is, the availability of consumption tracking may actuallyhelpthe firm. This possibility is evident in our next two examples (all derivations are provided in the appendix). Let𝛼=9/10,̃𝛼=25/28α=9/10,α˜=25/28, and𝑘=13/100k=13/100. Then, the availability of consumption tracking compels the firm to impose a penalty fee that would not otherwise exist, resulting in higher profits and lower consumer surplus. Example 1describes an unplanned equilibrium with several noteworthy properties. To begin, this equilibrium features a positive penalty fee, even though the firm would have set its penalty fee to zero in the absence of consumption tracking (𝜙*>0=𝜙*𝑁⁢𝑇ϕ*>0=ϕN⁢T*). Thus, the availability of consumption tracking compels the firm to impose a positive penalty fee that wouldnot otherwise exist. The reason that a positive penalty is optimal with—but not without—consumption tracking stems from the fact that the availability of consumption tracking can instill a false belief in a consumer that she will use the technology to avoid penalty fees. That is, a consumer may choose to consume att= 1—followed by the possibility of mistaken consumption and accrual of the penalty fee att= 2—based on this incorrect belief; if consumption tracking had not been available, the consumer would not have been confident in her ability to avoid penalty fees after consuming in the first period and would, therefore, abstain att= 1—in effect, pursuing abstinence-based avoidance of the penalty. By giving consumers a false sense of security—manifest as anincorrectbelief that they might use consumption tracking to avoid the penalty—the availability of consumption tracking can thus, rather ironically,preventconsumers from avoiding penalty fees in an unplanned equilibrium. As seen, the equilibrium inExample 1features higher profits and lower consumer surplus in comparison with the (zero-penalty) benchmark equilibrium that would arise without consumption tracking. Thus, unlike the planned equilibria considered earlier, here the availability of consumption trackinghelpsthe firm andhurtsconsumers. Although these effects may seem surprising from the standpoint that consumption tracking is supposed to help consumers avoid penalty fees, they are natural consequences of the fact that the availability of consumption tracking compels the firm to impose a positive penalty fee that would not otherwise exist. Indeed, the availability of consumption tracking necessarily leads to higher profits and lower consumer surplus in cases where the zero-penalty benchmark equilibrium would arise without consumption trackingandthe equilibrium with consumption tracking entails unplanned nonuse of the technology. Example 2is, in many ways, similar toExample 1. Although̃𝛼α˜is slightly lower, the other parameter values remain the same. Moreover, the availability of consumption tracking gives rise to the same unplanned equilibrium in both examples (𝜙*,𝑝*ϕ*,p*, Π,CS, and𝐷𝜏Dτdo not change). In contrast toExample 1, however, apositive-penalty benchmark equilibrium would now exist without consumption tracking.15 Even thoughExamples 1and2feature the same unplanned equilibrium, they can reflect qualitatively different effects of consumption tracking because the corresponding benchmark equilibria are different. For instance, the optimal penalty fee inExample 2is now lower with consumption tracking than without. Put differently, we are back to a “normal” situation in which the availability of consumption tracking compels the firm to reduce its penalty fee. With that said, the unplanned equilibrium inExample 2is still anomalous in other regards. Notably, the penalty fee and the subscription price now move in the same direction, with the firm lowering its penalty feeandits subscription price in response to the availability of consumption tracking. Here, it may seem counterintuitive that the firm would lower its subscription price even with a lower penalty fee. However, the availability of consumption tracking reduces consumers’ willingness to pay for a subscription—and by extension, the optimal subscription price—because consumers now (falsely) expect to incur the monitoring costk. Even though the firm inExample 2lowers its penalty fee and its subscription price, its profits still increase (as was the case inExample 1). Here, the firm can attain higher profits because consumers—guided by their false belief that they will use consumption tracking to avoid penalty fees—are more likely to consume in both periods (compared with the positive-penalty benchmark equilibrium), resulting in higher total payments of the penalty fee. Even so, this increase in profits doesnotcome at the expense of consumers. Instead, the availability of consumption tracking ismutually beneficial. Here, both profits and consumer surplus may increase because the contract generates more total value through consumers’ increased use of the service. Figure 5helps illustrate how the effects of consumption tracking on equilibrium profits and consumer surplus may vary with relevant parameter values inExamples 1and2. Here, the region with red vertical lines inFigure 5corresponds to the case where the availability of consumption tracking helps consumers but not the firm. The region with blue horizontal lines inFigure 5corresponds to the opposite case, in which consumption tracking helps the firm but hurts consumers, as inExample 1. The region with diagonal lines inFigure 5then corresponds to the case in which consumption tracking is mutually beneficial to the firm and to consumers, as inExample 2. Here, we can see how the reduction iñ𝛼α˜fromExample 1toExample 2leads to a “win-win” scenario, in which firm profits and consumer surplus both increase from the availability of consumption tracking. That said, further reductions iñ𝛼α˜(i.e., moving farther to the left of the purple dot inFigure 5) could lead to a scenario in which only consumers benefit. We can also now see the effect of changing the monitoring costkrelative to its value inExamples 1and2. Starting withExample 1(i.e., in relation to the blue dot inFigure 5), a decrease inkcould lead to a scenario in which consumption tracking is inconsequential (the light gray region inFigure 5), reflecting the fact that for these parameter values, the firm would not use a penalty fee regardless of the availability of consumption tracking. The effect of changingkinExample 2, however, is different. Higherkcan lead to a scenario where only the firm benefits from consumption tracking, whereas lowerkcan lead to a scenario where only consumers benefit. The availability of consumption tracking can only generate the following effects in an unplanned equilibrium(with̃𝐷𝜏>𝐷𝜏=0D˜τ>Dτ=0): an increase in the optimal penalty fee(𝜙*>𝜙*𝑁⁢𝑇ϕ*>ϕN⁢T*), higher profits(Π>Π𝑁⁢𝑇Π>ΠN⁢T),and lower consumer surplus(𝐶⁢𝑆<𝐶⁢𝑆𝑁⁢𝑇C⁢S<C⁢SN⁢T). Thus,in a planned equilibrium,the availability of consumption tracking can only lead to a lower penalty fee,lower profits,and higher consumer surplus(in cases where these values change). Previously, we saw how the availability of consumption tracking can, perhaps surprisingly, lead to a higher penalty fee (Example 1), higher profits (Examples 1and2), and/or lower consumer surplus (Example 1). FromCorollary 1, we see that each of these effects canonlybe realized in cases where the availability of consumption tracking gives rise to an unplanned equilibrium in which consumers never use consumption tracking, despite an initial belief that they would.16It is worth reiterating that an unplanned equilibrium—and by extension, the effects described inCorollary 1—can only arise withpartiallysophisticated consumers. This feature lends credence to the potential empirical viability of the unplanned equilibrium as partial sophistication is an empirically supported aspect of consumer decision making (see footnote 2 and the surrounding discussion). To relate our (entire) analysis of consumption tracking to observed practice, we may consider the evolution of personal banking in the United States. Seeing that overdraft fees have long been a staple of personal checking accounts, this market would naturally correspond to the positive-penalty benchmark equilibrium—along the lines described in part (ii) ofProposition 1—prior to the development of modern tracking technologies. The advent of online banking near the turn of the twenty-first century arguably represented a major innovation for tracking consumer spending.17Initially, however, online banking was not too popular, with adoption by only 18% of internet-using consumers in 2000; mobile banking apps (another significant innovation) also had a slow start, with the same 18% rate of adoption by 2011 (Fox 2013). Similarly, the now popular Mint personal finance app had only attained 1 million registered users as of 2009 compared with 29 million in 2021 (Beersheba Research 2021). Indeed, from around 2000 until the mid-2010s, modern tracking technologies for personal banking had yet to gain significant traction. In light of this, a firm would have had relatively little incentive to adjust its (positive) penalty fee in response to the availability of consumption tracking. Consistent with this idea, banks’ overdraft fees remained relatively flat during this time.18 Despite the relatively slow start, consumption tracking technologies have since become quite popular. By 2022, 89% of all consumers are using mobile banking apps (Yuen 2022), whereas the Mint app alone (as mentioned) had amassed 29 million registered users by 2021. During this time, there has also been a considerable shift from older web-based banking services to mobile apps that are optimized for easy use and convenient access (Centric Digital 2016). This latter trend suggests an overall reduction in the monitoring costkin addition to the significantly increased availability of consumption tracking in modern times. According to our analysis, a firm would generally reduce or eliminate its penalty fee askbecomes sufficiently small (Remark 3).19Consistent with these predictions, some banks have recently bucked long-standing practice by offering checking accounts without overdraft fees, whereas overdraft fees have declined considerably on accounts where these fees still exist.20 Lastly, much of our analysis focused on the possibility of an unplanned equilibrium. Empirical observation suggests that “unplanned nonuse” of consumption tracking may indeed be a characteristic of personal banking now that these technologies have become widespread. For instance, even though 89% of consumers are using mobile banking apps in 2022 (as noted), consumers seem to make little use of the tracking services offered by these apps. According to an Ipsos–Forbes study (Strohm 2022), only 5% of consumers identify “budgeting and tracking tools” as having been the most valuable feature of their mobile banking apps based on their use in the past year; this put “budgeting and tracking tools” as only the eighth most valued feature of such apps—well behind other features like “mobile check deposit” and “bill pay.” Similarly, of the 29 million registered Mint users (as of 2021), only 3.6 million actively use the app on a monthly basis (Beersheba Research 2021). Crucially, the fact that the inactive users once registered for the app lends credence to the idea that they intended to use the app but failed to follow through—consistent with our notion of unplanned nonuse.
article.5. Discussion of Model Variations and Ex: In the Online Appendix, we formally analyze several variations and extensions of our model. In this section, we summarize our main findings from these additional exercises and discuss which results of our main analysis are still attained. 5.1. Alternate Formulations of Consumer Forgetfulness The model underlying our analysis thus far features a rather particular specification of consumer forgetfulness, as described inSection 2. Namely, it is assumed that a consumer who consumed att= 1 will arrive att= 2 believing that there is a probabilityαthat consumption didnotoccur att= 1. Naturally, it may be worth considering other potential formulations of consumer forgetfulness.21 In the Online Appendix, we analyze five such alternatives: (i) a model in which consumers either completely forget or completely remember theirt= 1 consumption, withαbeing the share of such consumers who completely forget theirt= 1 consumption; (ii) a model in which consumers can recall theirt= 1 valuation of the service and use this to infer theirt= 1 consumption; (iii) a model in which consumers’ belief regarding their probability of consuming att= 1 is calculated based oñ𝛼α˜; (iv) a model in which consumers enter one of two “memory states” att= 2 and use Bayesian updating to infer theirt= 1 consumption probability conditional on their realized state; and (v) a model in which consumers aresymmetricallyforgetful of past consumption and past abstinence. As revealed by our analyses,allof these alternate formulations of consumer forgetfulness give rise to a rather stark prediction: that consumption tracking would beirrelevantas the equilibrium with consumption tracking is always identical to the corresponding benchmark equilibrium without consumption tracking. In other words, there are no market effects of consumption tracking under these alternate formulations of consumer forgetfulness. In light of this, it is evident that these other approaches (although appealing on other grounds) are not suited for studying the market effects of consumption tracking in markets with penalty fees. Recalling previous discussions (e.g., concerning U.S. banks’ responses to the widespread availability of mobile banking apps), we can also recognize that the predicted irrelevance of consumption tracking is not empirically supported. Moreover, all of these alternate models except for model (i) predict that the optimal penalty fee in equilibrium will always be zero—a prediction that is incompatible with the widespread use of penalty fees seen in practice. With all that said, another approach could be to allow heterogeneity inhowconsumers forget, with one segment adhering to the memory model used in our primary analysis and a second segment adhering to one of the alternate formulations discussed. With heterogeneity of this type, it will no longer be the case that a positive penalty fee can never be optimal or that the availability of consumption tracking can never lead to a new equilibrium. This fact is formally established in the Online Appendix for the case in which one segment is asymmetrically forgetful and the other segment is symmetrically forgetful (whereas the logic used to establish this fact would naturally extend to cases in which the second segment adopts one of the other alternate formulations of consumer forgetfulness instead). 5.2. Alternate Assumptions on the Timing of Decisions to Use Consumption Tracking Another key assumption in our main model is that consumers decide whether to use consumption tracking (if available) upon their arrival att= 2. This assumption is perhaps best suited to study technologies—such as mobile banking apps—that give consumers theoptionto check their past consumption at the time of the relevant consumption decision (and when the penalty fee may be incurred). With that said, this assumption is less compelling for some other types of tracking technologies, such as automated text alerts, in which the relevant choice (e.g., whether to register) is made well in advance. Although our main interest in this study is in understanding the market impacts of the former class of consumption tracking technologies (for which the relevant usage decisions would more naturally be modeled att= 2), it is still interesting—and relatively straightforward—to consider the latter class. With this in mind, in the Online Appendix, we explore three alternate assumptions regarding the timing of consumers’ decisions to use the consumption tracking technology. In one case, consumers decide whether to use the technology at the same time as theirt= 0 subscription decision. In the second case, consumers decide whether to use the technology upon their arrival att= 1 (i.e., immediately before learning theirt= 1 valuation). In the final case, consumers decide whether to use the technology at the end oft= 1 (i.e., immediately after thet= 1 consumption choice). As we saw with our alternate formulations of consumer forgetfulness discussed, the availability of consumption tracking proves irrelevant when we model consumers’ decisions to use consumption tracking as occurring att= 0 or at the beginning oft= 1. That is, in both of these cases, we find that the equilibrium with consumption tracking will always be identical to the benchmark equilibrium without consumption tracking. That said, allowing heterogeneity in consumers’ level of sophistication (i.e., iñ𝛼α˜) can restore the possibility that the availability of consumption tracking may lead to a new equilibrium in these cases (see the Online Appendix for formal demonstrations). Meanwhile, with decisions to use consumption tracking at theendoft= 1 (and going back to our original assumption that consumers are homogeneous iñ𝛼α˜), the availability of consumption tracking may still compel the firm to reduce or eliminate its penalty fee. That said, all equilibria will entail planned nonuse of consumption tracking, meaning that unplanned nonuse equilibria are no longer possible; as a result, the potential counterintuitive impacts of consumption tracking in unplanned nonuse equilibria—namely, an increase in the penalty fee, higher profits, and/or lower consumer surplus—are also no longer possible. Collectively, our analyses thus suggest that these potential counterintuitive impacts can only arise for the types of tracking technologies on which our study is mainly focused, referring to those that give consumers the option to recall their past consumption at the time the penalty fee may be incurred. More broadly, these analyses show that the timing of consumers’ decisions to use consumption tracking can be a critically important factor in determining the market effects of these technologies. 5.3. Other Model Variations and Extensions Some additional extensions and variations of our model are also considered in the Online Appendix. In one such exercise, we demonstrate that many of the predicted equilibrium outcomes of our model can still arise when consumers face heterogeneous monitoring costs (from using consumption tracking to remember past consumption). That said, some consumers may now actually use consumption tracking in the full equilibrium where the firm can optimally adjust its contract in response to the availability of consumption tracking. With heterogeneous monitoring costs, it is also possible that some consumers do not subscribe in equilibrium. As we find, however, the firm may be able to retain these otherwise unserved consumers—while earning higher profits—if we allow the firm to offer both a “premium” subscription with zero penalty fees and a “basic” subscription with a positive penalty fee (but a lower subscription price). In another extension of our model, we show that the firm will never use a positive penalty fee if subscriptions come with an “account freezing” feature that prevents a consumer from consuming when it would incur a penalty fee. Interestingly, if the presence of an account freezing feature is endogenously determined, it may be implemented by a social planner or regulator who seeks to maximize total welfare, even though market participants—consumers and the firm—would never implement such a feature if it were their choice. The reason for such a discrepancy is that consumers would (in these cases) benefit from an account freezing feature but fail to recognize such benefits as result of their distorted perceptions through̃𝛼≠𝛼α˜≠α.
article.6. Conclusion: This paper studies the market consequences of advances in consumption tracking technologies, including their effects on a firm’s marketing strategies and on the welfare of consumers. Importantly, consumers in our model may be forgetful about their past consumption and have limited sophistication about their future forgetfulness. These traits can make consumers especially susceptible to penalty fees, whereas consumption tracking technologies offer a potential means to mitigate the problem. Our analysis demonstrates that the availability of consumption tracking may help consumers directly as they can use the technology to avoid penalty fees. The availability of consumption tracking technologies may also indirectly help consumers—even those without access to the technology—by forcing the firm to reduce or even eliminate its penalty fees. Our analysis also reveals that the availability of consumption tracking may instill a false sense of security among consumers who are partially sophisticated about their forgetfulness. In particular, the firm may strategically set a penalty fee such that consumers expect to use consumption tracking to avoid penalty fees but ultimately, do not to bother. Notably, these consumers would have been more careful to avoid penalty fees (by abstaining from first-period consumption) if consumption tracking had not been available. In this way, the illusion of security created by consumers’ access to the technology can, ironically, make consumers more vulnerable to penalty fees while allowing the firm to increase its profits. The intuition for this effect is consistent with consumer overconfidence about future self-control generated by a cancellation option in a contract (DellaVigna 2009) as well as more general notions of excessive optimism with new technology (Clark et al. 2016). Our results may be of interest to managers and regulators. As we show, the firm’s optimal contract when consumers have access to consumption tracking will in general depend on the levels of consumer forgetfulness and sophistication. One challenge, however, is that these behavioral parameters may not be easily observed.22In light of this, our findings highlight the potential value to a firm of identifying and/or developing precise measures of consumer forgetfulness and sophistication. That said, even without precise measures, a firm may still be able to crudely and dynamically calibrate its penalty fee (and subscription price) if it finds that consumers are initially successful in avoiding penalty fees when new consumption tracking technologies become available. Even so, the firm’s ability to efficiently adapt its contract to such technologies would presumably be enhanced by a greater understanding of the levels of forgetfulness and sophistication among consumers in its market. For instance, a general sense of these parameters could still help the firm assess when or whether to “give up” on a positive penalty fee or whether it is possible to set a positive (and perhaps even larger) penalty fee that would induce an unplanned equilibrium in which the firm may earn extra profits by capitalizing on consumers’ false sense of security. In our analysis, we derive sufficient conditions—involving intermediate levels of consumer forgetfulness and (partial) sophistication—under which the availability of consumption tracking leads to an unplanned equilibrium characterized by higher profits and lower consumer surplus. Managers should also be attuned to any changes in consumers’ costs of using consumption tracking technologies and anticipate how the penalty fee, in addition to being a tool to exploit consumer forgetfulness, may affect consumers’ use of consumption tracking. Despite recent advances in consumption tracking technologies, these costs (whether monetary or nonmonetary) remain significant for many consumers. For example, using a mobile app to monitor one’s account balances can entail access costs (e.g., the cost of a smartphone), data storage costs (e.g., for a smartphone with limited memory), learning costs (e.g., to learn how to use the app), and mobile data usage costs as well as costs related to the time and attention required to use the technology. The use of these technologies may also involve data security concerns; after all, giving a third-party app access to personal transaction information can have serious consequences. Even with a relatively low cost of using consumption tracking, our analysis suggests that a consumer may not use a consumption tracking app because of the firm strategically setting its penalty fee at a level for which consumers lack a sufficient incentive to use the technology. In total, we can see that many hurdles must be overcome for a consumer to make regular use of the app. For regulators, encouraging new consumption tracking technologies makes intuitive sense as a way of helping forgetful consumers remember their past consumption to reduce unnecessary penalty fees. However, the effects of such measures may not be as straightforward when taking into account the costs of using these technologies and the firm’s strategic responses. Regulators should, therefore, carefully assess these factors. Overall, the potential benefits to consumers from advances in consumption tracking technologies will not be significant until the costs of using these technologies have decreased substantially. To further understand the effects of consumption tracking technologies, future research may also examine the role of competition—a factor that is not accounted for in our monopoly model. It is conceivable that the levels of consumer forgetfulness and sophistication could affect consumers’ choices among service providers, whereas the availability of consumption tracking technologies may affect the intensity of price competition between service providers. On the empirical side, future research may develop hypotheses based on our analytical results and test these hypotheses with observed behavior. For instance, empirical work may investigate the variations in the penalty fees during the diffusion process of a new tracking technology or after the introduction of new regulations that affect the use or availability of consumption tracking technologies. At the consumer level, empirical research may also help assess whether (and if so, the extent to which) access to consumption tracking technologies creates an “illusion of security” among consumers.
